title: The Road Not Taken 1

jokes.text: Not only has your card risen to the top of the deck, but it has changed into the ace of diamonds.
jokes.setup: Jim promises to make Latka's card magically rise to the top of the deck
jokes.punchline: The card has risen but changed into a completely different card, making the trick a failure
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection - sets up expectation of successful magic trick, delivers spectacular failure instead

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Your card has risen to the top of the deck.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But that's not my card!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Well, it rose to the top, didn't it?

jokes.text: Always leave them wanting more.
jokes.setup: Jim's magic trick was a complete failure
jokes.punchline: He delivers the classic performer's exit line as if he succeeded
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim character moment - delusional confidence in the face of obvious failure, delivered with professional performer's poise

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Thank you, you've been a wonderful audience!

jokes.text: Well, that's one strike against it.
jokes.setup: Tony mentions he has friends in Seattle
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately frames this as a negative for the city
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's nastiness - turns what should be a positive into an insult against Tony

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I have friends in Seattle.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's too bad for Seattle.

jokes.text: Flatten the bum. / What? / Flatten him. / Oh, that makes more sense.
jokes.setup: Tony mishears 'flatten' as 'flatter' in boxing advice
jokes.punchline: The clarification reveals the absurdity of the original mishearing
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay setup with Tony's characteristic confusion, followed by logical resolution

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: You gotta flatten your opponent!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Flatten? I thought you said flatter him!

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: No, flatten - knock him down!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh. Well that makes a lot more sense than telling him he's got nice eyes.

jokes.text: Why would go out and flatter someone you were gonna fight? / Psychological ploy? / Oh, I guess if he's wearing a particularly nice pair of shorts, it couldn't hurt to mention it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of why you'd compliment a boxing opponent
jokes.punchline: Tony's earnest suggestion about complimenting the opponent's shorts
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of absurdity - Tony takes the ridiculous premise and makes it even more ridiculous with sincere boxing advice

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Why would you compliment a boxing opponent?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You could compliment his shorts. Tell him they're nice shorts.

jokes.text: Gee, no wonder Jack didn't want you to talk to me. You're so discouraging.
jokes.setup: Frank just told Tony he's going to lose the fight
jokes.punchline: Tony treats serious intimidation as mere pessimism
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony character moment - his innocent response to obvious mob intimidation shows his endearing naivety

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Lose the fight? Hey, that's the kind of negative thinking that's gonna kill ya. You gotta believe in yourself, Frank. You gotta have confidence. You walk in that ring thinking you're gonna lose, you've already lost.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Tony, the guy outweighs me by fifty pounds.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So what? I fought a guy once who outweighed me by a hundred pounds.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I lost. But I went in there thinking positive!

jokes.text: Are they right very often? / Oh, wait, don't tell me, man. This could shake my confidence.
jokes.setup: Tony asks about the betting accuracy of mob enforcers
jokes.punchline: He immediately realizes this might not be good for his morale
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Tony's slow realization process and self-preservation instinct kicking in

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what the odds are that a mob enforcer's bet is accurate?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wait... I probably shouldn't know that.

jokes.text: Hey, you know, you're the kind of guy that gives Italians a bad name. / I'm not Italian. / Well, do they know you're doing this?
jokes.setup: Tony assumes Frank is Italian based on stereotypes
jokes.punchline: When corrected, Tony wonders if Italians know about Frank's behavior
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection and character work - Tony's innocent logic about ethnic responsibility is both subversive and endearing

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Frank, you're Italian, right? You must know all about the good life - wine, pasta, la dolce vita.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I'm not Italian, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're not? Well, then how come you don't know anything about wine or cooking?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Because I'm not Italian.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you look Italian. You act like... Wait, if you're not Italian, what are you?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I'm Jewish.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Jewish? How come you never told me? You know, I gotta ask - do the Italians know you've been impersonating them all these years?

jokes.text: Better manager? What do I need a better manager for? I got you, Jack. When it comes to training fighters, you wrote the book, bud.
jokes.setup: Tony defends Jack as a great manager
jokes.punchline: Sets up for Jack to deflate Tony's confidence in his methods
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the subsequent running gag about Jack's lack of innovative training methods

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Jack is a great manager. He knows how to handle people.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Thanks, Tony. I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, you really know what you're doing. You've got a real talent for this.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Well, Tony, if I'm so great, how come you're still driving a cab?

jokes.text: How about, how about running for stamina? / I hate to burst your bubble, kid, but, uh, that's older than jumping rope.
jokes.setup: Tony tries another example of Jack's expertise
jokes.punchline: Jack admits running for stamina is even more basic
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the running gag, escalating the deflation of Tony's faith in Jack

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So Jack, you run for exercise?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Yeah, I run every morning.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What do you run for — speed or distance?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Neither. I run for stamina.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Stamina? That's even more basic than I thought.

jokes.text: How about improving the speed of my punches by shaving my forearms? / You mean, my wind resistance theory?
jokes.setup: Tony brings up an absurd training method
jokes.punchline: Jack takes credit for this ridiculous 'wind resistance theory'
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the running gag - the one thing Jack claims credit for is completely absurd, showing his incompetence perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I read about this new training method where you run backwards into the wind.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Backwards into the wind? That's brilliant! I've been saying that for years.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You just heard about it thirty seconds ago.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm a quick study.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be with you all the way, in spirit. / What do you mean 'in spirit'? I mean, ain't you gonna be in my corner?
jokes.setup: Jack promises to support Tony 'in spirit'
jokes.punchline: Tony realizes this means Jack won't actually be there
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jack's cowardice and Tony's dawning realization of abandonment

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You mean you're not coming?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I'll be there in spirit.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: In spirit? Jack, I need you in body!

jokes.text: Use a pay phone. I'll call you.
jokes.setup: Jack tells Tony to call him after the fight
jokes.punchline: Jack's paranoia about phone tapping reveals how scared he is
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Jack's paranoia and complete abandonment of Tony in his hour of need

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Call me after the fight.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Jack, what are you talking about? It's just a friendly sparring match.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But use a pay phone. And don't mention my name. They're listening to everything.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Who's 'they'?

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: You know who. The people. They tap phones. They follow you. Last week I saw the same taxi twice on the same street.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Jack, that's... that's a taxi company. We work for a taxi company.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, I'm glad you're gone. I don't need your help anyway. I don't need nobody's help. I'll win this fight alone with nobody's help.
jokes.setup: Tony tries to act tough after Jack abandons him
jokes.punchline: His repetitive insistence reveals his desperation and loneliness
jokes.explanation: Touching character moment - Tony's bravado quickly crumbles into obvious need for support

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I don't need Jack! I'm tough, I can handle myself!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm tough! Real tough! You hear me? Tough!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I don't care that he left. I'm fine. I'm really fine. Everything's fine.

jokes.text: Hey, I feel sorry for you, man. You're fighting an angry man with a cause.
jokes.setup: Pre-fight trash talk between boxers
jokes.punchline: Tony frames himself as righteous rather than intimidating
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Tony's naive attempt at intimidation focuses on moral righteousness rather than physical threat

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: You know, I'm not here to scare you or intimidate you. I'm here because what you did was wrong, and somebody's gotta stand up for what's right.

jokes.text: You're going down hard tonight, man. / Tony Banta don't take no dives for nobody. / I know, man. That's why they got me to take the dive.
jokes.setup: Typical pre-fight intimidation and defiant response
jokes.punchline: Wilkes reveals he's the one taking the dive, not Tony
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - completely subverts the expected dynamic and Tony's moral stand becomes meaningless

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think you can beat me? I'm tougher than you'll ever be!

dialogue.speaker: Wilkes
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, I've got news for you, pal. I'm the one taking the dive tonight, not you.

jokes.text: You're a disgrace! / I know, man, but tag me a good one so I can go down, okay?
jokes.setup: Tony's moral outrage at Wilkes taking a dive
jokes.punchline: Wilkes pragmatically asks Tony to help make the dive look good
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the absurd situation - Wilkes's practical approach to corruption contrasts with Tony's moral stance

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're gonna take a dive? How could you do that? That's dishonest! That's immoral!

dialogue.speaker: Wilkes
dialogue.line: Tony, Tony... will you help me make it look good?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: How much?

jokes.text: No, man, I don't want no part of this. / Come on, man, I need the dough!
jokes.setup: Tony refuses to participate in the fixed fight
jokes.punchline: Wilkes begs Tony to hit him so he can take his dive
jokes.explanation: Continues the role reversal well - now the corrupt fighter is desperate and Tony has the moral high ground

dialogue.speaker: Wilkes
dialogue.line: Come on, Tony, just hit me. Just one punch. I need to take the dive.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No. I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Wilkes
dialogue.line: Please, Tony. I'm begging you. My career depends on this.

jokes.text: How do you live with yourself?
jokes.setup: Wilkes is down for the count in the boxing ring
jokes.punchline: Tony uses this moment to lecture him about ethics
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony character moment - his moral earnestness is both admirable and completely inappropriate for the moment

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, Wilkes, lying there on the canvas, you got time to think about things. About right and wrong. About integrity. You can't go through life taking shortcuts, cutting corners, doing whatever it takes to win. That's not a life—that's just existing. You gotta have principles, standards. You gotta be able to look yourself in the mirror and know you did things the right way, even if it cost you.

jokes.text: Think you should wait. Think you should hang in there, stay cool, and sooner or later, even though he says he's not going to, Alex is gonna tell you what to do.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks Tony for advice about her job decision
jokes.punchline: Tony's advice is to wait for Alex to make the decision despite Alex refusing to help
jokes.explanation: Good character insight - Tony understands the group dynamic and Alex's inevitable involvement

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Tony, what should I do?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You should wait for Alex to decide.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: But Alex said he won't help me decide.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So? You wait anyway.

jokes.text: Nardo, you're wasting your time listening to Profiles in Sewage here.
jokes.setup: Tony just gave his advice to Elaine
jokes.punchline: Louie dismisses Tony with a play on 'Profiles in Courage'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Louie insult - clever literary reference twisted into a putdown that's both mean and witty

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Profiles in Cowardice.

jokes.text: I have been looking for something to fill the void ever since my last canker sore.
jokes.setup: Jim wants to hear Louie's story
jokes.punchline: He compares the entertainment value to having a canker sore
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Jim logic - finding entertainment value in medical discomfort shows his unique perspective

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Louie, tell us that story about the time you were in Atlantic City.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, there was this time when I was down at the track and I met this dame...

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, Louie, listening to your stories is about as much fun as having a canker sore.

jokes.text: Hey, what were you doing, Louie? / I was a cabdriver.
jokes.setup: Louie describes his lowest point with a menial job beneath his dignity
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was doing the same job everyone else does now
jokes.explanation: Excellent irony - Louie considers cab driving beneath him while surrounded by cab drivers, perfectly capturing his arrogance

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I hit rock bottom once. Had to take a job so degrading, so humiliating, I couldn't even tell people what I did.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What was it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was a taxi driver.

jokes.text: A little slice of heaven. A piece of my wife's peach pie.
jokes.setup: Tom offers something to lift Louie's spirits
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's homemade pie, showing his earnest kindness
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment - shows Tom's genuinely caring nature that contrasts with Louie's cynicism

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Louie, I made you something to lift your spirits.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Homemade pie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: ...You made this?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah. I thought it might cheer you up.

jokes.text: Top o' the morning to you, Louie. / Cram it up your bagpipe.
jokes.setup: Philippe gives a cheerful Irish greeting
jokes.punchline: Louie responds with a crude Irish-themed insult
jokes.explanation: Good Louie character moment - takes Philippe's ethnicity and throws it back as an insult with creative vulgarity

dialogue.speaker: Philippe
dialogue.line: Top of the morning to you!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well kiss my Irish ass!

jokes.text: Your wife's peach slop gives me the trots.
jokes.setup: Tom offers Louie his wife's peach pie again
jokes.punchline: Louie crudely rejects it with TMI about digestive issues
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie crudeness - takes Tom's kindness and responds with gratuitous oversharing and insults

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Louie, my wife made her famous peach pie. Can I interest you in a slice?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Forget it, Tom. The last time I ate that pie, I spent three days on the can. My intestines staged a revolt. I was passing things I didn't even eat.

jokes.text: A pair or better to open, Lydia.
jokes.setup: Lydia suggests strip poker
jokes.punchline: Tom tries to set poker rules for what's clearly a sexual proposition
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Tom's innocent attempt to impose order on Lydia's chaos shows his sweet naivety

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: How about a game of strip poker?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, I don't know. What are the stakes?

dialogue.speaker: Lydia
dialogue.line: Oh, come on, Tom. You know the stakes.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, but let's establish some ground rules first. No wild cards, five-card draw only, and we play for minimum ante.

jokes.text: Oh, you don't know how relieved I am.
jokes.setup: Tom mentions his sister's doctor visit is nothing serious
jokes.punchline: Louie sarcastically expresses fake relief about someone else's health
jokes.explanation: Good Louie sarcasm - perfectly captures his self-centered worldview and lack of genuine concern for others

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's wonderful. I'm so relieved. Now I can sleep tonight.

jokes.text: Now, why would a person want to blast their voice over a loudspeaker when they could talk to them very...
jokes.setup: Louie questions the need for the PA system
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself by using it loudly
jokes.explanation: Good setup and immediate payoff - Louie's hypocrisy is revealed within the same sentence

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do we need this PA system for anyway? Nobody uses it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: MARIO! GET IN HERE! NOW!

jokes.text: is gonna be squandering his precious talent sitting in a cage doing a job a chimpanzee could do.
jokes.setup: Louie announces he'll be taking over dispatch
jokes.punchline: Describes the job as so simple a chimpanzee could do it
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic self-own - Louie insults the job he's about to take permanently, revealing his lack of self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm taking over dispatch. This job is so simple a chimpanzee could do it.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You're doing great so far.

jokes.text: How's that? / Yeah, who's gonna bring the Bromo?
jokes.setup: Tom offers to make Brown Betty for the company picnic
jokes.punchline: Louie suggests they'll need stomach medicine
jokes.explanation: Continues Louie's pattern of insulting Tom's wife's cooking, maintaining character consistency

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'll make my famous Brown Betty for the company picnic.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Great. Make sure you bring plenty of Pepto-Bismol.

jokes.text: Did he say he'd wipe your nose for you, too? Beat it! This job stinks enough without being a baby-sitter.
jokes.setup: Philippe asks for the cab with the heater for his cold
jokes.punchline: Louie treats basic accommodation as coddling
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment of Louie's management style - cruel and unreasonable from the start

dialogue.speaker: Philippe
dialogue.line: Louie, I have a terrible cold. Could I please have the cab with the heater?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The heater? What do you think this is, a luxury liner? You want heat, wear a sweater.

jokes.text: Five bucks for a cab with a heater?
jokes.setup: Philippe asks for accommodations for his illness
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately starts charging for basic comfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment - instantly corrupts his position of power for personal gain

dialogue.speaker: Philippe
dialogue.line: Louie, I am not feeling well. Could you perhaps find me a comfortable place to rest?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A comfortable place? That'll be five bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Philippe
dialogue.line: But Louie, I am ill!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Ill? That's another five bucks. And if you want a pillow, that's separate.

jokes.text: Thanks for spoiling the ending, Reiger!
jokes.setup: Alex reveals how Louie's story ends
jokes.punchline: Louie breaks the fourth wall to complain about narrative spoilers
jokes.explanation: Nice meta moment - Louie treating his life story like entertainment and complaining about spoilers

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: In the end, Louie dies alone in his office, surrounded by his filing cabinets.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! Hey! What are you doing? You can't just tell people how my story ends! That's a spoiler!

jokes.text: He became a crosswalk guard.
jokes.setup: Concern about what happened to Tom after retirement
jokes.punchline: Louie reveals Tom's 'success' story with characteristic dismissal
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - presents career destruction as no big deal with typical Louie callousness

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You wanna know what happened to Tom? He's doing great. Real great. He bought himself a little place down in Florida, he's sitting on the beach all day, drinking piña coladas, got himself a nice tan.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The guy's livin' the dream.

jokes.text: These brownies are great. I'm really stoned.
jokes.setup: Gordon eating brownies at a college party
jokes.punchline: Directly states the obvious effect with no awareness
jokes.explanation: Basic stoner humor - Gordon's oblivious directness about drug effects, functional but not inspired

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: These brownies are amazing! I feel so relaxed and happy right now.

jokes.text: Gordon... Gordon, you're not crying. / W-Well, what am I doing? / You're smiling and happy.
jokes.setup: Gordon thinks he's crying from emotion
jokes.punchline: Jim has to explain Gordon is actually happy
jokes.explanation: Good stoner confusion - Gordon's complete disconnection from his own emotional state

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Gordon, you're crying.

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: I know. I'm very moved.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, Gordon. You're happy. That's why you're crying.

jokes.text: Yeah, if the next guy is a Quaker.
jokes.setup: Jim claims he likes to party as much as the next guy
jokes.punchline: Heather suggests the comparison group is very restrictive
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference insult - perfectly captures Jim's uptight nature with specific religious comparison

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I like to party as much as the next guy.

dialogue.speaker: Heather
dialogue.line: Yeah, but the next guy is a cab driver named Lou who goes home and watches TV.

jokes.text: Congratulations, Gordon, that was almost a complete sentence.
jokes.setup: Gordon attempts to make a philosophical point while high
jokes.punchline: Jim sarcastically congratulates Gordon's basic communication
jokes.explanation: Good Jim sarcasm - his academic precision applied as an insult to Gordon's drug-addled communication

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: You know, man, when you really think about it, like, we're all just floating through space on this rock, and none of it really matters, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wow, Gordon, congratulations. You've just discovered philosophy. What's next, are you going to tell me that fire is hot?

jokes.text: I forgot my thought!
jokes.setup: Gordon trying to make a deep philosophical point
jokes.punchline: Complete mental breakdown mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Standard stoner humor - the classic 'lost my train of thought' gag, competently executed but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: You know, the fundamental question of existence is whether we are merely passengers in the cosmic machinery of fate, or whether our consciousness allows us to transcend the predetermined...

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: ...and when you really think about it, the dichotomy between free will and determinism creates a paradox that... that... SQUIRRELS! Why do squirrels always know where they buried their nuts?!

jokes.text: You've been feeding us walnuts?!
jokes.setup: Heather lists brownie ingredients including marijuana
jokes.punchline: Jim focuses on the walnuts as the concerning ingredient
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - Jim's priorities are completely backwards, focusing on nuts instead of drugs, perfectly capturing his character

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Walnuts? You're putting walnuts in brownies?

jokes.text: Gordon, this is our room. / I thought the ceiling looked familiar.
jokes.setup: Gordon acts like he discovered Jim and Heather's location
jokes.punchline: Reveals he recognized his own room by the ceiling
jokes.explanation: Good stoner logic - Gordon's complete spatial disorientation leads to recognizing his own space as unfamiliar

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: I found them! I tracked them down to a seedy motel on the edge of town.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How did you find us?

dialogue.speaker: Gordon
dialogue.line: I recognized the ceiling. It's my room.

jokes.text: I get high on life. I very high on life. As a matter of fact, I'm high right now!
jokes.setup: Jim claims he doesn't need drugs to have fun
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic declaration reveals he's clearly affected by the brownies
jokes.explanation: Good escalating irony - Jim's straight-edge philosophy crumbles as the drugs take effect, revealed through his own enthusiasm

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I don't need drugs to have fun! I can have just as much fun without them!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Look at me! I'm flying! Wheeeee!

jokes.text: first you nag me into premarital sex. / Okay, I'll give you that.
jokes.setup: Jim lists things Heather has corrupted him with
jokes.punchline: Heather casually accepts responsibility for sexual corruption
jokes.explanation: Good relationship humor - Heather's matter-of-fact acceptance of Jim's moral concerns about their relationship

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You've corrupted me with cigarettes, you've corrupted me with alcohol, you've corrupted me with all kinds of things.

dialogue.speaker: Heather
dialogue.line: Well, I haven't corrupted you sexually.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yet.

dialogue.speaker: Heather
dialogue.line: Yet.

jokes.text: I'm fine. Now, let's go study.
jokes.setup: Jim has clearly been affected by the marijuana brownies
jokes.punchline: Maintains his academic focus despite being obviously high
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency - even while high, Jim tries to maintain his studious persona

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You know, it's fascinating when you really think about it. The molecular structure of cannabis affects the cannabinoid receptors in the brain, which is why I'm experiencing these cognitive... these really interesting sensations right now. But I digress. As I was saying about the epistemology of Kant...

tmdb_overview: When Elaine is offered a job as an art gallery manager in Seattle, she asks the gang for help in making a decision. The cabbies share with Elaine some stories about life changing decisions they once had to make. Tony talks about how he refused to take a dive in a major fight, even though he will likely be in trouble with the mob. In the fight, Tony learns that the other fighter has also been paid to take a dive, and the fighter does so in comic fashion. Next, Louie talks about how he became a taxi dispatcher after mercifully taking over for the kindly old dispatcher who had to take his wife to the doctor. Louie finds he likes the job and forces the old man out of the dispatcher's job into retirement. Finally, Jim tells of how he was very studious at Harvard, until his free-spirit girlfriend turned him on to hash brownies. (Part one of two)
insight: Forty-five jokes in 45 minutes—Taxi maintains its steady rhythm despite middling impact scores.
